raw_premise: is there any point to have more extra fingers past one extra artificial finger?
sharpest_question: Point for whom, doing what task, measured how — the premise has no user, no task, and no outcome variable.
claim: Adding supernumerary robotic fingers beyond one yields diminishing or negative returns on task performance, cognitive load, or embodiment — but the premise does not yet commit to which.
differentia: Not visible. The premise does not name a task domain, a dependent measure, or a comparison condition that would distinguish it from existing supernumerary-limb studies.
nearest_template: Supernumerary robotic limb / Third Thumb work (e.g., Dani Clode / Makin lab at UCL; MIT's supernumerary robotic fingers by Asada's group). These studies test single extra digits for bimanual-task augmentation, grasp stability, and motor cortex reorganization.
missing_evidence: No user population specified (able-bodied augmentation? amputees? surgeons? musicians?).
No task specified — cardinality of fingers only matters relative to a manipulation demand.
No dependent variable — performance, learnability, embodiment, neural representation, cognitive load?
No control condition — is the comparison 1 vs 2 vs N extra fingers, or extra fingers vs other end-effectors?
No mechanism hypothesis — why would a second extra finger help or hurt (DOF saturation? control bandwidth? body-schema capacity?).
No actuation or control modality specified — fingers driven by which body signal (EMG, foot, breath, residual toe)? Control channel scarcity may be the real bottleneck, not finger count.
sharpened_options: Territory: able-bodied manual augmentation is bottlenecked not by end-effector count but by available control channels. Niche: existing supernumerary-finger studies (Third Thumb, MIT SRF) test N=1 extra digit and do not characterize the control-channel ceiling. Occupation: in a bimanual assembly task, performance gains saturate or invert between one and two extra fingers when control is mapped to independent toe-flexion signals, because users cannot allocate independent motor commands beyond ~1 extra DOF cluster.

Territory: body-schema plasticity for non-biological limbs. Niche: prior work shows embodiment of a single extra thumb but has not tested whether the body schema can incorporate multiple simultaneous supernumerary digits. Occupation: subjective embodiment and proprioceptive drift measures will decrease monotonically as supernumerary finger count increases from 1 to 3, indicating a capacity limit on schema extension.

Territory: task-specific manipulation where the human hand is geometrically insufficient (e.g., surgical knot-tying, luthiery, cable routing). Niche: supernumerary-finger literature has focused on generic grasping, not geometrically-constrained tasks where added fingers have a principled role. Occupation: in tasks requiring simultaneous contact at >5 points, two extra fingers produce measurable completion-time gains over one, whereas in <=5-contact tasks they do not — the value of extra fingers is task-topological, not general.

Territory: rehabilitation for partial hand loss. Niche: prosthetic research optimizes replacement of missing digits but has not examined whether adding beyond anatomical count restores or exceeds baseline function. Occupation: for users with 3-finger residual hands, restoring to 5 outperforms restoring to 4, but adding a 6th yields no ADL performance gain — establishing an anatomical ceiling rather than a control ceiling.
